data.title: Rough Ride Exacting A Toll
data.description:
Preliminary estimates show that the University of Michigan’s consumer sentiment for the US plunged to 50.8 in April - below forecasts of 54.5 - and the lowest level since June 2022. Consumer sentiment has fallen 40% since November 2024 (below since 1970, courtesy of The Financial Times).

At the same time, tariff announcements drove year-ahead inflation expectations to 6.7% (from 5%), the highest reading since 1981. See Consumer sentiment tanks in April on recession fears, and it’s not only consumers who are expressing angst about the outlook:

“Consumers report multiple warning signs that raise the risk of recession: expectations for business conditions, personal finances, incomes, inflation, and labor markets all continued to deteriorate this month,” survey director Joanne Hsu said.

Friday’s survey is the first major read on consumer sentiment since President Trump’s April 2 “Liberation Day” announcement of sweeping tariffs, which triggered a steep market selloff. The survey period ended April 8, the day before Trump announced a 90-day pause on some tariffs.”

According to the Bank of Canada’s Q1 Business Outlook Survey, business investment and hiring intentions have weakened considerably, with employment plans falling below pandemic-era lows. Thirty-two percent of firms surveyed expect a recession in the next 12 months, up from 15% in the previous quarter. March employment data reinforced these concerns with the job count falling and unemployment rising.

While most are banking on lower interest rates this year, a recent backup in Treasury yields has increased financing costs for the public and private sectors. The US 10-year Treasury yield leapt 63 basis points (BPS) from 3.86% on April 4 to 4.497% on April 11.

Companies borrow at a premium or spread rate above similar-dated Treasury bonds. Last week, high-yield spreads (companies with BB+ and worse credit ratings) rose to 461 bps, the highest since the March 2023 regional banking crisis (below since 2015).

So far, the Treasury market has been more attentive than the equity market. The last time corporate borrowing costs were this high, the S&P 500 was at 3800, some 29% lower than last week’s close.

With the US economy in decline, the Fed is expected to cut interest rates more in 2025, and both of those factors tend to weaken the greenback. Year to date, the US dollar has weakened sharply against major trading partners.

At the same time, as the US buys fewer goods from foreign countries, those countries receive fewer greenbacks and need to raise cash from other places to pay their bills. They have less to recycle into longer-duration financial assets, like stocks, corporate debt, precious metals, cryptocurrencies and treasuries. See The Dollar and the Bond Market’s Ominous Message for Donald Trump.

This is where liquidity needs can accelerate into a financial crisis. Some aggressively levered funds were forced to liquidate holdings after their positions went against them last week. And there will likely be more of that to come.

As asset prices fall, credit conditions tighten, just as fears about tariff inflation could dampen central banks’ reaction functions.

New Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent has said his office is focused on extending the term of the Federal debt to lower interest costs and extend payback periods. For that to happen, America needs the bond market to believe that inflation is under control and that lower interest rates are sufficient compensation for lenders.

As yields leapt into last Wednesday, President Trump announced a 90-day pause on additional tariffs, explaining that “People were getting a little queasy.” Queasy is an understatement.

Once entrenched, history warns that such negative financial momentum is difficult to reverse.

Disclosure: No positions.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Jet Set Go: Why I'm One Of Boeing's Biggest Bulls
data.description:
Introduction

The aerospace industry has been one of the fastest-growing industries in modern history. Ever since commercial aviation went mainstream after the Second World War, it has enjoyed elevated secular growth from people's desire to go places.

Initially, growth mainly came from North America and Europe. Now, the biggest growers are Asian nations, which are experiencing a booming middle class. Meanwhile, developed markets are still growing consistently, as access to commercial aviation keeps improving.

Even better, this entire industry is dominated by just two players.

Imagine having a duopoly in one of the most favorable industries on the planet...

Despite this, The Boeing Company (NYSE:BA) is in what could become a period of seven consecutive years of losses, as it started losing money in 2019 due to 737 MAX issues, followed by the pandemic and a wide range of operational problems amplified by industry-wide supply chain issues.

As a result, over the past ten years, Boeing has returned just 16%, trading miles below its 2019 peak levels, while the S&P 500 has more than doubled during this period. Its European peer, Airbus (OTCPK:EADSF), has returned 168%.

With that said, there's light at the end of the tunnel.

For starters, the company's stock price has bottomed. Since September 17, when I wrote my most recent article on the company, it has remained flat despite a 5% decline in the S&P 500. That's not bad.

Moreover, it has won the NGAD contract, which is now named the Boeing F-47. As much as I wanted Lockheed Martin (LMT) to win it, as it's one of my biggest investments, it's another step in the right direction for Boeing.

Hence, I'll use this article to update my thesis and explain why Boeing remains a great risk/reward play for people who can stomach drama and volatility.

So, let's get to it!

Boeing's Big F-47 Win

On March 21, Boeing was officially awarded the NGAD (Next Generation Air Dominance) contract for engineering and manufacturing development. It was a competition between Boeing's Phantom Works and Lockheed's famous Skunk Works.

Boeing won based on best overall value, which is an approach that looks into items that go beyond cost, as the Air Force has figured out that going with the lowest bidder isn't always the best way to get value.

Anyway, what's interesting is that despite all the chatter that drones may make jets irrelevant, we're not seeing these fears in government spending. This makes sense, as I have often written that fighter jets are more than just flying machines that can shoot rockets.

This NGAD program is a flagship initiative for the Air Force, which has been envisioned as a "family of systems" centered on a manned sixth-generation fighter.

Essentially, the F-47 will come with advanced stealth, extended range, supercruise capability, and integration with drones and space assets to provide air superiority against peer adversaries like China and Russia.

In other words, it's a flying multitasker.

Having said all of this, the NGAD is the company's first win as a prime contractor since the 1990s, when its merger with McDonnell Douglas got it the deals for the F-15 and F/A-18 jets.

Now, Boeing needs to prove it is still up to the job, as it dealt with multi-billion-dollar cost overruns on programs like the KC-46A tanker and the Air Force One. This is on top of the many problems it had/has in its commercial segment.

The good news is that the NGAD contract is structured as a cost-plus-incentive-fee deal, which mitigates financial risk by allowing the government to cover overages while rewarding Boeing for meeting performance targets.

To add some numbers, the EMD phase of the contract (which Boeing just won) is valued at more than $20 billion. This includes options for low-rate initial production and a path to full-scale manufacturing.

As the cost of an F-47 jet is estimated to be close to $300 million, the total contract value could be hundreds of billions of dollars. This number could improve dramatically if foreign nations are allowed to purchase these jets as well. The F-22, for example, was not open for export.

The first production planes could leave the factory in the early 2030s, which means we're likely 5-10 years away from finding out just how successful this program can become.

For now, it's a big win, as it adds revenue stability and innovation capabilities (with DARPA support) that could result in more wins down the road.

This could include the F/A-XX, the successor of the F/A-18. After Lockheed has been eliminated from the contract, it could go to either Boeing or Northrop Grumman (NOC).

According to Defense News, this program is a major step for the Navy to increase its range and add new capabilities like AI.

F/A-XX will include new capabilities and technologies, such as artificial intelligence and machine learning, Rear Adm. Michael Donnelly said at the Navy League’s Sea Air Space conference. The upgrades will provide more battlespace awareness and improve how naval aviators make decisions.

Those technological advancements could help bring the Navy into a new era where piloted and unmanned aircraft operate more closely together, such as with the Navy’s planned AI-operated drone wingmen, known as collaborative combat aircraft, or larger, unmanned platforms that might come in the future. - DefenseNews

Although my personal opinion is that it is unlikely that a company wins two major deals in a row, it could add even more stability to Boeing's outlook, especially as growing defense operations tend to reduce cyclical risks of the entire Boeing business.

More Good News On A Path To Recovery

Last month, the company used the Bank of America Global Industrials Conference to update investors on progress in major programs. This includes the 737 program, which remains on track to achieve a production rate of 38 units per month, supported by inventory buffers for critical components like fuselages and engines.

This is good news, especially in light of the current tariff turmoil.

Additionally, thanks to resolved supply chain issues in the 787 program, it is looking to produce seven units per month next year, up from five units per month in 2025.

These operational improvements aren't just important in general, but because the company is sitting on a backlog of $435 billion, which covers more than 5,500 planes.

To further streamline operations, the company is working with the FAA to improve metrics like rework levels, traveled work (tasks moving between stations), supplier shortages, employee training compliance, defense escapes, and issue ticketing.

With regard to tariffs, it helps that 80% of its commercial and 90% of its defense procurement is domestic. Aluminum and steel account for just 1-2% of aircraft costs. On top of that, certain costs are hedged.

Moreover, by "mid-2025," Boeing aims to fully complete the takeover of Spirit AeroSystems, which is a former spin-off of Boeing and the world's largest producer of fuselages and other critical supplies.

According to the company, reintegration aims to improve safety, streamline decision-making, and align long-term investments, especially in 737 MAX fuselage production.

Culturally, Boeing plans to fully absorb Spirit employees "as if they never left," with immediate rebranding and leadership integration.

It also helps that the 777X remains on track for first delivery in 2026.

Debt Issues & Shareholder Value

One of Boeing's biggest problems is debt, which has soared due to financial losses.

At the end of last year, the corporation had $53.9 billion in debt, with nearly all of this being long-term debt.

In October 2024, Boeing raised $24 billion through a capital raise, which included a $10 billion credit agreement and plans to raise up to $25 billion through stock and debt offerings.

Moreover, last year, Fitch gave Boeing's new notes a BBB- rating. While this is still an investment-grade rating, the outlook is now negative, which means any headwinds could cause Boeing to lose its investment-grade rating.

The Negative Outlook reflects Boeing's heightened execution risks over the next 12 months to 24 months as it eliminates legacy inventory, stabilizes and increases aircraft production rates, navigates seasonal cash flow volatility, pursues additional corporate actions that should enhance longer-term operations and fulfills its commitment to repay debt and return to IG metrics. - Fitch Ratings (emphasis added)

That's the bad news.

Now comes the good news.

This year, net debt is expected to come in at $35.7 billion. That's gross debt minus cash. It would imply a 9.2x leverage ratio, which is abysmal.

However, that's due to very low EBITDA. Through 2027, net debt is expected to fall to $20.5 billion, just 2.3x EBITDA.

While Boeing may have become a 'mess' in recent years, it still has everything it needs to hit it out of the park, which Fitch recognized as well:

The company is one of two major global aircraft manufacturers with substantial diversification to differentiate it from its largest competitor, particularly given its large defense and services businesses. Fitch projects the company will continue to generate positive annual FCF throughout its rating case with potential to approach double digit billions by the end of 2027 as operations normalize and operating margins improve. - Fitch Ratings (emphasis added)

By 2027, free cash flow is expected to reach $9.0 billion, which would translate to nearly 8% of its market cap. Currently, it would give BA the highest free cash flow ratio of all major aerospace companies on my radar.

Applying its historical P/FCF ratio of roughly 15x, we get a fair market cap of $135 billion, roughly 15% above the current price.

To me, that's the base case.

We see something similar when using EPS expectations.

Using the FactSet numbers from the chart below, the street is looking for $6.86 in 2027 EPS, with 2026 being the first profitable year since the 737 MAX issues. Applying a 22x multiple, we get a fair price target of $150, which is a few dollars below the current price of $156.

While that may seem slightly bearish, I believe BA will get a much higher multiple once it becomes profitable.

If Boeing manages to become profitable next year and generate more than $9 billion in 2027 free cash flow, the market will reward this handsomely, as the next few years could see higher operating efficiencies, income from the 777X program, higher 737/787 output, and support from the NGAD/F-47 program.

Although it's fair to say that there are still substantial operating risks, I really like the risk/reward and would be a buyer of Boeing stock at these levels, if I did not already own five companies in this industry.

Takeaway

Boeing's story is messy, but full of potential.

After years of setbacks, the company is finally showing signs of a turnaround with major wins like the F-47 NGAD contract, improving commercial operations, and a roadmap to generate strong free cash flow.

Yes, risks remain, especially with debt and execution, but if Boeing stays on track, I believe the payoff could be substantial.

If I didn't already own five aerospace stocks, I would be adding BA here.

data.title: TSMC: Deeply Discounted AI Buy
data.description:
Business is not slowing down for Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (NYSE:TSM) and probably isn’t going to in 2025.

TSMC gave investors a sneak preview of its first quarter sales accomplishments on Thursday and the chip company is profiting from an extremely resilient demand picture for its chips.

In my last stock update on TSMC I pointed to the chip company’s growing net sales on one hand, and the potential for higher margins in 2025 on the other.

With Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company now reporting very robust sales growth of more than 40% YoY, as well as a QoQ acceleration, I think that TSMC is a highly compelling investment for investors that want to add chip companies to their portfolios while they are on sale.

TSMC’s Sales Update For 1Q25

Based on TSMC’s latest sales update, published on April 10, 2025, the chip company produced March sales of NT$285.96 billion, up 10% compared to the prior month and up 46.5% compared to the same month a year ago.

Sales between January and March totaled NT$839.25 billion, reflecting an increase of 41.6% YoY. Though sales growth accelerated 10 percentage points compared to February 2025, TSMC did not report its highest monthly sales (both October 2024 and January 2025 brought in higher monthly sales).

That said, with the chip company obviously profiting from enormous pent-up demand for the utilization of its manufacturing capacity, it seems that it will be only a question of time until Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company will report new record monthly sales.

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company sales growth is accelerating, as the company’s sales report showed unequivocally. March sales rose 46.5% YoY and since December 2024, TSMC’s sales growth consistently accelerated for three months straight.

The trajectory, in terms of YoY growth, is highly beneficial and with demand for AI chip manufacturing being as high as it is now, I think that Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company is poised to make a very compelling earnings presentation for 1Q25 on April 17, 2025 that could possibly showcase higher margins in TSMC’s manufacturing business.

Based on a collection of 4Q sales data by trendforce, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company is the uncontested leader in the foundry industry. In 4Q24, according to trendforce, TSMC captured 67.1% of foundry sales and profited from the biggest sales increase (14.1%) on a QoQ basis.

With Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company being the biggest foundry in the world, accounting for 2/3rds of the foundry market in terms of 4Q sales, no other foundry is better positioned to take advantage of incrementally higher demand for AI chips than TSMC. In my view, the robust market position makes the case for margin upside in 2025. Taking into account that TSMC is also the lowest priced chip company in the peer group, I think that the low valuation based on profits makes TSMC a no-brainer ‘Buy’.

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company is going to make its earnings presentation on April 10, 2025 which, if the sales report is any indication, is probably going to be a really good one. The most important metric to watch, in my view, will be Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company’s gross margins which I anticipate to have grown QoQ as well. With a 10% month-on-month jump in net sales in March 2025, there is a good chance that the chip company was able to profit from higher prices for AI chip production as well.

In the prior quarter, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company had a [GAAP] gross margin of 59% and the chip company has forecasted a margin of 57-59% for the first quarter of 2025.

With demand for AI chip manufacturing clearly re-accelerating this year, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company is poised to report robust gross margins for 1Q25 as well and I would not be surprised for the chip company to beat its own margin forecast next week.

TSMC Is In The Bargain Zone, Strong Buy Classification Is Very Much Justified

Despite its leading market share in the foundry market, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company is the cheapest chip company that investors can buy. Of course, this has something to do with investors fearing that Taiwanese chip production could potentially fall under the control of the Chinese Communist Party as well as that the threat of looming tariffs on Taiwanese semiconductors could threaten TSMC’s imports into the U.S.

TSMC is selling for 14.7x next year’s estimated profits which are anticipated to clock in at $10.77 per share. The implied annual profit growth rate for Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company is 18.4% which is the lowest profit growth anticipated of any chip company in the peer group. The highest profit growth is expected to happen for Intel Corp. (INTC) which is presently reorganizing its business.

Nvidia Corp. (NVDA) and Advanced Micro Devices Inc. (AMD) are selling for profit multiples of 20.0x and 15.6x and are both bargains, in my book, particularly because TSMC’s considerable sales upswing in 1Q25 is related to growing demand for chip production.

Since Nvidia and AMD utilize Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company’s manufacturing facilities to produce chips on a contract basis, TSMC’s sales growth implies that chip design companies like Nvidia and Advanced Micro Devices are also poised to see substantial tailwinds for their sales and profits in 1Q25.

ASML Holdings N.V. (ASML), which profits from surging bookings for its lithography systems, is presently the most expensive chip company in the peer group, with a leading Price-To-Earnings Ratio of 22.8x.

Taking into account that all chip companies have seen quite considerable valuation haircuts as of late, I have a ‘Strong Buy’ classification on the chip sector. In my view, TSMC presents the best value here for investors, from a valuation angle, and I think we could see substantial re-rating in 2025.

A 20x profit multiple (leading to an implied intrinsic value of $216) could be a realistic valuation target, in my opinion, primarily because TSMC is one of the most critical companies in the semiconductor supply chain.

Why The Investment Thesis Might Be More Bearish

New macro risks have popped up as of late and the most notable is a potential recession in the United States, particularly if China and the United States keep imposing tit-for-tat retaliatory tariffs on each other.

In addition, tariffs on Taiwanese companies like TSMC could possibly lead to lower export volumes and thus slowing sales and profit growth for the chip company.

Moreover, growing competition in the market for custom-made computer chips could possibly be a long-term challenge for contract manufacturers like Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company.

My Conclusion

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company’s sales report for 1Q25 paints a very rosy picture for the chip company ahead of 1Q25 presentation next week, but also for the AI chip manufacturing industry at large.

While the recent tariff tantrum has shaken up investors and dominated the headlines, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company keeps growing at a blistering pace and there is no indication that this growth is poised for a slowdown in the near-term.

TSMC just realized its third straight quarter of accelerating sales growth, which also points to the potential for a stronger-than-expected gross margin in 1Q25.

TSMC is also considerably undervalued to its other chip peers, particularly considering that the manufacturer owns the market for chip production. Buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Kingstone: Clean Turnaround But A Lot Of Value Already Priced In
data.description:
Property and Casualty insurer in the Northeastern US, Kingstone (NASDAQ:KINS) has been a rare stable stock unaffected by the recent turmoil in equity markets. The stock has a lot of intrinsic value, a deep value buy for a high-risk reward profile, fundamentally speaking, as we will see further on. However, I start cautious given the last year’s run up in prices.

The Turnaround Story in 2024

After three consecutive quarters of profitability, KINS ended FY 2024 with a Net Income of $18.4m after years of struggling with operational headwinds and actuarial losses on catastrophic events. The Kingstone 3.0 strategy was aimed at addressing the issues plaguing the business by reducing presence in non-primary markets, repricing product offerings and cost reduction efforts. So far, it seems to have worked. Improvements in actuarial models are also in play – although catastrophic events risk is relatively untested. Something like the six wind events in Q3 2023 alone, is the kind of tail risk that may dampen earnings.

The EPS has shown a regime change and turned healthy, signaling a turnaround. Free cash flow has also made significant improvements in 2024, enabling investments in technology and modeling research. The RoE stands at 27% as a result of the turnaround in income in 2024. What is also significant is the drop in total debt to equity ratio, fueled by reducing debt and increasing equity. Debt prepayments from stock sale have been pivotal in this revival. All the improvement has also coincided with Kingstone’s new strategy, Three Point O - it is working.

Direct Premiums written in 2024 surged by 20% to $240m. The Expense Ratio remained roughly around the 33% mark during this growth, while the net loss ratio improved from 72.4% in 2023 to 48.7% in 2024.

Management guidance is positive and the expectation is to maintain a similar growth rate in Direct Premiums written at a similar Combined Ratio as seen in 2024. Marginal improvements in EPS and RoE are expected.

Cost Reduction and Core Efficiencies

On the cost side, Kingstone has been extremely efficient. There is a reported 25% reduction in debt and a sale of its headquarters to consolidate the balance sheet. KINS has shown a good amount of focus on keeping costs lean on staffing –move to remote working, more reliance on independent contractual sales network and optimization of the commission structure has shown KIN’s commitment to keep its expenses light and agile. KINS has also migrated from legacy systems to unified technology platforms and focused on talent acquisition in the past few quarters, leading to significant cost reduction impacts.

Kingstone achieved an underwriting expense ratio reduction YoY from 36% to 32% in 2023. However, it did miss its target of further reduction to 30% (clocked 33%) in 2024. I don’t see a significant deviation of note here, particularly for a milestone year in terms of turning profitable and rising Premiums Written leading to higher performance bonus costs among others.

Changes in the reinsurance policy is also a material development, with modifications in coverage limit and reduction in first event retention. Underwriting discipline and improvements has been a strong focus pillar in the recent past. Moving to tiering of risk policies by risk quantum perceived, stricter underwriting standards and improved risk profiling has been a priority and has started to yield results - in terms of lower claim frequency and lower claim severity. Improved underwriting is also visible in changes from the windfall losses in Q3 2023, where catastrophe losses were at 7.7%, to 1.7% in Q3 2024. Exiting the high loss states of Florida and Texas has also helped KINS to focus in its key strength geographies.

Valuation and Price Action

At some point last year, KINS was trading at a deep discount of less than 1 forward P/B ratio. Today, the forward P/B ratio is 2.35. According to Seeking Alpha, the sector median is 0.99. According to a research paper in Stern School of Business, the Property and Casualty Insurance sector average P/B is 2.33, KINS is certainly no longer the deep value buy it was a year back. Price action indicates that I may have missed the bus over the past year.

Risk Reward Signals a Hold

Putting the story together, the basic checks are all good. There is a lot to like about KINS. Kingstone is a legacy business dating back years and has a niche presence in the Northeast. Wrapping up new geographies and refocusing on its stronghold is directionally encouraging. Improved efficiencies and increase in business at lower expense costs are great signs for a turnaround. It is also great to be evaluating a stock without worrying about tariff repercussions, although any generic economic downturn risk will exist.

On the risk side, catastrophic risk still exists. Although, fundamentally, I like the underwriting discipline and reinsurance changes roped in by KINS, the improvement in catastrophic loss reduction in Q3 2024 vs Q4 2023 is not yet convincing enough as the prior period was a tail risk exposure event, not seen in 2024.

KINS has run up significantly over the last year on the back of improving fundamentals. Deep value investors who invested at that time would have reaped the upside very well. KINS still retains interest as its niche market and now cleaner balance sheet is a potential acquisition target for larger players. However, the risk reward profile is not rewarding at this point. KINS may go further up from here - it wouldn't be a surprise, given the clean revival in business and financials. However, I believe valuations have moved up from the value buy zone to fairly priced and entries now will be less prudent than a year back.

My take on KINS is a partial profit booking for existing investors or “Hold”. New investors with an appetite for risk and volatility (which has dropped in the past few months, to be honest) can consider a Buy just because of its improving fundamentals. Seeking Alpha’s Quant Rating indicates a Strong Buy on profitability, momentum and considerable analyst earnings revisions (turnaround from Hold to Strong Buy in Oct 2024). However, I don’t recommend fresh entries at this point beyond small portfolio exposure on informed speculation lines.

data.title: I See Richardson Electronics Building Quiet Momentum
data.description:
Thesis

Richardson Electronics, Ltd. (NASDAQ: RELL), which began in precision power components and electron tubes, has become a global solutions provider, now supporting mission-critical industries such as semiconductor manufacturing, renewable energy, and advanced imaging. As I look at the company today, what I see is a business at something of an inflection point, gradually letting go of its legacy baggage and focusing its efforts on higher-margin, growth-oriented markets. And while the road ahead depends, as it usually does, on a mix of operational progress and external uncertainties, it does seem that Richardson is steadily shifting from being a traditional supplier to becoming a more distinct engineering partner. The real question now, of course, is whether they can keep up this momentum long enough to turn potential into lasting profitability.

Signs of Momentum Building

Richardson Electronics today sits at an inflection point in its ecosystem. What struck me immediately in the Q3 quarter earnings report is how Richardson continues to thread the needle between niche resilience and mainstream relevance.

Revenue inched up 2.73% year-over-year to $53.8 million, a figure that marked their third consecutive quarter of growth, an important signal of momentum. But underneath that headline, you find the real pulse of the business. Semiconductor wafer fab sales shot up 139% over the prior year, a breakout performance that confirms Richardson’s critical role in the semiconductor manufacturing supply chain, particularly as U.S. reshoring policies and global supply chain realignments take hold. In the same breath, Canvys, their custom display unit, posted a sharp 39.5% sales increase, riding demand from medical OEMs and industrial markets alike, while the Power & Microwave Technologies [PMT] group, their legacy strength, still managed to post 6.6% growth.

These kinds of operational successes point directly to how Richardson is positioning itself in a broader ecosystem populated by giants and faltering rivals alike.

Teledyne Technologies (TDY), which has been outperforming the market this year, continues to validate the growth story in high-reliability RF and microwave applications. Teledyne’s strength effectively raises the tide for the sector, creating downstream demand that benefits Richardson’s engineered solutions.

On the other hand, while MACOM Technology Solutions (MTSI) keeps pouring money into its operations and growing its semiconductor footprint, the whole competitive field is still a moving target. That leaves room for Richardson to quietly scoop up extra business as customers scramble for supply chain security. In this kind of environment, Richardson’s steady operations and clean balance sheet really do stand out. They’ve now racked up four straight quarters of positive operating cash flow, pulling in $4.6 million just this quarter, and free cash flow swung to $4.1 million, which is a nice change from burning through $2.5 million a year ago. With a spotless $36.7 million in cash and no debt to worry about, they’ve built themselves a reputation for reliability that is hard to argue with.

Mild Margin Expansion

Margin expansion adds another layer to this positive ecosystem alignment. Consolidated gross margin widened by 150 basis points to 31%, with PMT improving to 30.8% and Green Energy Solutions, arguably their most strategically exciting segment, jumping to a healthier 32.8%. In a climate where cost pressures are everywhere, it’s clear that Richardson’s deliberate pivot to higher-margin products is paying dividends, both operationally and philosophically.

Historically, their healthcare business was a direct play in the aftermarket parts space, but with the sale of their Fort Mill operation to DirectMed Imaging, they’ve stepped back from direct competition to play a smarter game. DirectMed, turbocharged by Frazier Healthcare Partners’ private equity firepower, is aggressively consolidating the Canon/Toshiba aftermarket and rapidly expanding its global reach.

Rather than challenge DirectMed head-on, Richardson has taken the role of essential backend supplier. Through an exclusive supply agreement, they maintain distribution rights for in-demand Siemens Straton Z and ALTA750 X-ray tubes, effectively embedding themselves deeper into DirectMed’s supply chain.

Lingering Pain Points

Of course, the quarter wasn’t without its bruises. Despite operational wins, Richardson reported a net loss of $2.1 million, with EPS of negative $0.15, missing consensus estimates by a wide $0.23 margin. The Healthcare asset sale, while strategically sound, left an ugly mark on the quarter with a $4.9 million loss from asset disposals. Layered within that were painful write-offs: $1.4 million in excess components, $1.1 million in intangible assets, and continued drag from the soon-to-be-sunset ALTA tube line, which management admitted will likely remain a loss-maker well into fiscal 2026.

Green Energy Solutions, while flashing promise with a 26% year-to-date sales increase to $23 million, wasn’t immune to volatility either. Sales in the segment dipped 19% year-over-year this quarter due to the inherently lumpy nature of wind turbine battery module orders. This remains a watch item, though I’d argue it's not a fundamental flaw, just timing noise typical of project-based revenue streams. Similarly, Canvys, despite strong revenue growth, experienced margin compression from 34.4% to 33.2%, raising mild concern over pricing or input cost pressures.

External risks, of course, are still part of the story. It's no surprise that Tariff uncertainties have made customers more hesitant, pushing out orders and leaving Richardson to juggle its inventory. Management was clear about this, pointing out that only 5% of purchases are tied to China, but even so, the situation has led to a wait-and-see approach among some of their customers. Under-absorption in manufacturing is still a problem too, with fixed costs running ahead of current production, though management expects this to ease as order volumes pick up.

The final wrinkle is visibility.

Despite strong backlogs, $95 million between GES and PMT, management struck a cautious tone on U.S. market growth, which they expect to be limited in calendar 2025. Semiconductor demand visibility beyond Q4 is also blurry, and while M&A remains on the table, leadership suggested it may not materialize until fiscal 2026. In the meantime, losses from Healthcare will linger, and the race to scale Green Energy and Power & Microwave will be a critical execution test.

Expanding Market Potential

On one hand, the structural tailwinds pushing behind RELL are genuinely powerful. The RF components market, central to nearly every part of their portfolio, from grid infrastructure to next-gen semiconductor applications, is expanding rapidly. Forecasts point to industry growth from $35.55 billion in 2024 to over $41.1 billion in 2025, a 15.6% CAGR that is hard to ignore. All this demand is being driven by the never-ending sprawl of wireless communications, the global frenzy to build out telecom infrastructure, and, of course, the military’s fondness for radar and satellite gear. Richardson’s Power & Microwave Technologies division is parked right in the middle of this spending stream, and they’re fully aware of it.

Add to that the growth in RF and microwave power transistors, which are expected to rise from $7.27 billion to over $12.1 billion by 2034, and Richardson’s addressable market starts to look even better. These are components where Richardson plays both roles: a commodity supplier and an engineering partner, offering high-efficiency, high-reliability solutions that meet the tough standards of aerospace, defense, and telecom customers.

Their ULTRA3000 ultracapacitor modules, built to take over from the clunky old lead-acid batteries in wind turbines, are a textbook case of Richardson looking ahead. With wind energy investment picking up speed worldwide and plenty of aging turbines ready for an upgrade, this looks like a pretty solid growth driver. The backlog, now at $95 million across Green Energy and Power & Microwave, shows they’re getting some real traction. And while management is, quite sensibly, keeping a cautious tone, they’re clearly aiming to scale these businesses enough to make a proper dent in earnings.

Valuation Trajectory

That optimism is echoed in the valuation trajectory. Right now, fiscal 2025 forward-looking estimates peg Richardson at 31.35x P/E, which at first glance looks aggressive. But it’s important to understand what the market is pricing. This is not about a cyclical trough valuation that assumes recovery. Analysts expect that as operating leverage returns and growth initiatives like Green Energy Solutions and semiconductor orders ramp, the multiple will compress sharply to 10.72x by 2026, stabilizing around 14.82x in 2027, a level that feels much more in line with historical averages and sector peers.

Consensus sees 192.31% EPS growth in fiscal 2026, which on the surface feels outsized, but given the distressed earnings base they’re climbing out of, I think it’s a reasonable snapback expectation.

And beyond the noise, the longer-term outlook calls for a 25% EPS CAGR over the next three to five years, comfortably ahead of the sector median of around 14%.

What keeps me cautiously optimistic is the fact that the cash flow is actually holding up. Operating cash flow shot up 609.29% year over year, and, as I noted earlier, free cash flow swung firmly into the black. The company is showing it can squeeze real cash out of operations, even when the reported earnings look a bit messy. With that $37 million in cash and no debt to worry about, Richardson has both breathing room and spare fuel, the sort of thing most small-cap industrials can only wish for. Their enterprise value is sitting at $82.81 million, which, given the cash they’re generating and the growing backlog, feels fairly modest.

Management’s careful approach to capital allocation only adds to the case. They’ve cut capex by 45.51% year over year, not because they’re panicking, but as a deliberate pause to make sure they can actually see the growth coming. Future spending is lined up to speed up time-to-market for new Green Energy products and to boost capacity for fast-growing semiconductor components.

Final Takeaway

Based on the full picture, I’d call RELL a "Buy." The current profitability looks a bit of a mess, thanks to healthcare asset sales and some transitional write-offs, but the growth in high-margin areas like semiconductors and Green Energy is picking up speed. Now down around 42% YTD, with expanding markets, a sharp turnaround in free cash flow, and earnings expected to bounce back in fiscal 2026, I think this is a decent spot to start dipping your toes in the water for meaningful upside, even if, to be fair, the near-term picture is still foggy.

data.title: Inside Nebius's Secret GPU Strategy
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Since my last coverage on Nebius Group (NASDAQ:NBIS), the stock has pulled back roughly 16%, offering investors a chance to accumulate shares near the recent $21 private placement price secured by institutional backers like Nvidia (NVDA), Accel, and Orbis. I’ve continued digging deeper into the company to better understand its strategic edge and evolving position in the AI infrastructure race. Today, I’m sharing fresh insights and findings from that ongoing research, uncovering the structural advantages that could make Nebius one of the most overlooked GPU infrastructure plays in the market.

How Nebius Quietly Built a Cost Advantage Over CoreWeave

Nebius’s business model is defined by vertical integration, geopolitical arbitrage, and hyperscaler-grade efficiency without hyperscaler-grade expenses. By designing, owning, and operating its whole AI infrastructure stack, including its data centers, custom server design, liquid-cooling systems, and GPU supply chain, Nebius is not reliant on third-party providers or artificially inflated GPU rental marketplaces like the majority of its competitors.

Not only does it reduce operational costs (e.g., sub-$0.025 per GPU-hour in Serbia), but it also raises uptime, customization, and sovereignty to its users. Its deliberate management of power, thermal design, and rack density allows it to keep GPU clusters at near-peak utilization rates, and that matters to maintain margins on inference and training workloads.

Nebius provides the lowest possible price in the GPU cloud market, facilitated by its capital position. Nebius minimizes gross margins through their ODM approach down to around 2%, which greatly minimizes initial CapEx on hardware and operational costs, for instance, for electricity. This cost-effectiveness positions Nebius in a position quite different from that of other providers that are not hyperscalers, as they utilize optimization that one only finds in hyperscale cloud providers.

A further comparative examination by PoolCompute reveals that Nebius AI has a very competitive pricing plan that may retain up to 50% of the money that users pay as opposed to leading cloud providers. For example, Nebius has Nvidia H100 GPUs available for $4.85 per hour pay-as-you-go, with deep discounts for longer periods of commitment, down to $3.15 per hour for a 12-month reservation. CoreWeave, on the other hand, has a very flexible and economical costing plan with a wide variety of Nvidia GPUs, with up to $4.76 per hour for the H100 HGX.

These results indicate that Nebius’s cost-per-FLOP will be around 40–60% less than its peers, such as CoreWeave (CRWV), making Nebius a low-cost option for GPU cloud services when customers commit for extended periods, such as 12 months. In other words, Nebius can do the same AI work as others like CoreWeave but for about half the cost. This means they’re much more efficient and can offer cheaper, faster services.

Thus, when public pricing for H100s sits around $2.00 per hour, a 2.5-cent operating cost shows just how wide the margin potential is, positioning Nebius to scale profitably once CapEx is absorbed.

The model has its imperfections, though. The biggest threat is capital intensity and long payback horizons. Proprietary data centers, GPU sourcing from gray markets, and custom firmware stacks all have high capital expenditures up front; Nebius spent nearly $2 billion in three years in CapEx alone. It delivers performance upside but raises the threat if demand weakens, sourcing streams dry up, or geopolitical tides change direction. It limits enterprise growth in the West from countries in or subject to sanctions, and many Nebius customers fall into that category. It lowers the addressable market and may curb growth in the long term.

Despite all the risks involved, the moat that emerges will be real. If Nebius continues to lock up GPUs at sub-market prices, offers sovereign AI services outside U.S. jurisdiction, and adds to its AI developer platform with offerings like Toloka and TripleTen, it will build meaningful switching costs. With data residency, sovereignty, and cost being just as relevant in a bifurcating world of AI as raw muscle, Nebius may not need to match the scale of AWS or Google (GOOG) but simply needs to own the alternative lane.

Why the Market Needs GPU-as-a-Service (GPUaaS)

The recent explosion in generative models like ChatGPT, Stable Diffusion, and custom enterprise-grade LLMs has triggered a gold rush in GPU demand. But most research laboratories and research centers and even mid-sized organizations cannot afford and maintain clusters of H100s or the next Blackwell chips. Not only do they come at a high cost, with prices well in excess of $30,000 per chip, but they require high-end cooling systems, high-throughput networks, and skilled engineers to service and maintain them. That’s where GPU-as-a-Service (GPUaaS) fits in.

GPUaaS allows companies to “lease” access to high-end GPUs on-demand without having to pay up-front CapEx or costly infrastructure buildouts. It is particularly crucial in a world where it costs millions to train high-end models. Instead of investing in hardware, developers can focus on model development, data quality, and deployment. The GPUaaS architecture provides the compute foundation for the AI economy, scalable, agile, and capital-efficient to the client.

Along with that, hyperscalers like Microsoft (MSFT) Azure or Amazon (AMZN) AWS bundle GPU access with long-term contracts, usage lock-ins, and cost premiums. It presents a chance to GPUaaS providers like Nebius to offer faster onboarding, transparent costs, and better performance per dollar. With demand for AI in all sectors from pharmaceutical research to autonomous systems on the rise, the GPUaaS layer becomes a requirement and not an extravagance.

This is why the GPUaaS market will rise to ~$100 billion in the next several years, with much of the growth going to those who can offer predictable throughput, sovereign data ownership, and cost-effectiveness. Whoever possesses the most performant, adaptable, and cost-effective GPU layer in that game will have the future in its hand in terms of the AI infrastructure and Nebius is positioning itself as one of the only serious contenders outside the hyperscaler oligopoly.

The New Cloud Goldmine

These examples illustrate why GPUaaS with its high up-front cost will be worth it in the end. Look at AWS (Amazon Web Services) in the early 2000s, a capital-intensive gamble. Amazon invested in massive data centers and abstracted computing into an on-demand service provided to developers. Today the internet runs on AWS and provides more than 60% of Amazon’s operating income. That model, owning and monetizing compute capacity, turned CapEx into high-margin recurring revenue. Nebius’s GPUaaS play follows the same script with a twist focused on AI compute and not generic cloud.

Another example in point is Nvidia itself. Once a gaming GPU firm only, Nvidia made significant investments in CUDA programming software and tools and in partnerships within the ecosystem. The initial investment created a self-sustaining cycle where there were increasingly more and more models built on Nvidia hardware, locking in demand. The data center business is now the largest and fastest-growing business at Nvidia based on the GPUaaS-like dependence that workloads in AI have on its platform.

These examples show that investing in the “picks and shovels” of the next tech cycle, whether cloud computing in the 2000s, AI in the 2020s, or the next thing, can be very profitable if timing and execution go in your favor. Nebius aims to recreate that model in sovereign and developing markets where demand precedes the hyperscalers and trust in U.S.-controlled infrastructure is dwindling.

Infrastructure Innovation: Embracing Liquid Cooling to Enhance Efficiency

Nebius stands apart through ownership and optimization of physical space, a departure from the typical industry model based on third-party colocation data centers. Adopting the models used by Baidu and Tencent, Nebius has developed proprietary high-density data centers, several of which have been constructed underground to be space- and thermal-efficient. A feature of the facilities is the application of high-end liquid cooling systems.

Nebius data centers in Russia and Serbia are among the region’s most GPU-optimized data centers due to the emphasis on liquid cooling technology. With 45U custom racks with direct-to-chip cooling plates and extremely low air gaps, the data centers have near-peak GPU utilization with no thermal throttling risks involved. The architecture not only increases performance but also results in significant cost reductions in operations. The liquid cooling cost will be up to 30% lower in operational costs within a five-year time frame even with intensive AI training workloads.

Furthermore, Nebius real estate and electrical infrastructure ownership protects the company from fluctuations in the cost of power, an issue in European and U.S. data center markets that will grow in saliency in the future. The vertical integration strategy underscores Nebius’s commitment to cost containment and operational efficiency and places the company in a favorable position compared to competitors with higher operating costs due to facility leasing.

AI Service Expansion: From Infrastructure to Sovereign AI Solutions

In addition to its hardware developments, Nebius is expanding its footprint in the space of AI services with the development of an end-to-end stack of AI that matches and in multiple places exceeds that offered by Western hyperscalers. The company has tuned models such as LLaMA 2 and Falcon to support Cyrillic and Balkan languages to fill a significant void in regionally targeted AI solutions. Nebius is developing Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) APIs designed to enhance the accuracy and contextuality of AI-generated content to meet the needs of users in the region.

A central element of Nebius’s strategy is its focus on providing secure AI-as-a-service solutions that allow customers to run inference workloads across sovereign or classified data without exposure to foreign jurisdictions. Its “red-teamed sovereignty stack” features containerized LLMs, air-gapped orchestration, and post-quantum encryption layers, analogous to early deployments by companies like Palantir (PLTR) but with an emphasis on in-country control. The strategy has attracted customers like Russia and Eastern European state-affiliated universities and defense contractors and established a regional market for AI development.

Takeaway

As the AI arms race evolves into one focused on survivability and local control, Nebius offers a compelling model for sustainable growth. Its innovative approaches to GPU sourcing, infrastructural design, and delivery of AI services not only differentiate it from the pack but lay a strong foundation upon which to expand in the years to come. To investors and market observers alike, Nebius is a lesson in the manner in which agility, forward vision, and technological innovation can coalesce to produce a strong competitor in the market for AI infrastructure.

data.title: Prada Buys Versace From Capri: Who Gains The Most?
data.description:
In my last article on Prada S.p.A. (OTCPK:PRDSF) I emphasized how solid this company was and how much it was undervalued compared to its fair value. Towards the end, I briefly mentioned the possibility of buying the Versace and Jimmy Choo brands, and I was quite excited about it. Those rumors have now become official, and Prada has entered into an agreement with Capri Holdings to buy Versace for $1.37 billion; Jimmy Choo will not be part of the agreement. But who stands to gain more between the two companies?

Prada has excellent reasons for buying Versace, but at the same time it seems that Capri Holdings Limited (NYSE:CPRI) won't be weakened by the agreement. In this article I'll show you the point of view of the two companies, and who I think will gain the most.

Capri Holdings: pros and cons of the sale

From a purely economic point of view, the sale of Versace for $1.37 billion cannot be considered a success in any way given that the Italian brand had been purchased for $2.10 billion in 2018. A loss of $730 million in 7 years that becomes even more significant if we consider the opportunity cost.

Since it was purchased, the Versace brand has gradually deteriorated, leading to an operating loss and double-digit sales decline.

It is clear that Capri Holdings did not manage to make the most of it, and a series of mistakes were made in its management. The objective was to reposition the brand for a new growth cycle after disappointing years, but this was not achieved.

We also made missteps in some of our repositioning efforts. While elevating the assortment, we believed we removed too many unique Versace statement items. Additionally, we significantly reduced our offerings of products at entry-level luxury price points. This impacted our retail sales, but had a more significant impact on our wholesale business.

John D. Idol - Chairman and CEO.

The decision to reduce entry level price points was not appreciated by consumers, and the crisis in the entire sector has fueled negative sentiment towards Versace. Demand for luxury is driven mainly by China, and since the latter is still overcoming the real estate crisis, there is a lack of lifeblood for this industry. In such a complex period, only brands with a strong competitive advantage can continue to grow, and Versace has not proven to be among them. Furthermore, the presence of a constant operating loss suggests that the brand has never really integrated itself within the synergies of the company.

In the last quarter, Versace sales represented only 15% of the group's total sales, while Michael Kors sales were 72%; the latter is the only brand that has seen an operating profit in the last quarter. Management is much more focused on the latter, but we are talking about a totally different market compared to Versace. The former deals mainly with handbags in the medium-to-high price range, while the latter is a high-end, long-standing brand that makes a wide range of products.

A few months ago, management's desire to join Tapestry was a clear signal of its new focus: to dominate the medium-to-high-end handbag market. The goal was to gain control over the two most popular brands in this price range, Coach and Michael Kors, but the authorities did not approve the deal. Versace is a heavy legacy to support, and Capri Holdings has not been able to profit from it as it did with Michael Kors.

Selling a brand for $730 million less than what you paid for it leaves a bitter taste, but at this rate Versace would have depreciated even more. It's understandable that Capri let it go, and with the incoming cash it will be able to both invest more in its main brand (Michael Kors) and improve its financial position. Its net debt is $2.76 billion and its operating income has proven to be rather unstable in recent years.

All in all, it's not an ideal situation from a financial point of view, but the $1.37 billion in proceeds will make it more stable and able to invest in the long term. In addition, Versace's operating loss, which weighs on the net profit, will be removed.

So, on the one hand Capri Holdings is losing a historic brand that didn't perform as hoped, but on the other hand it will improve its financial structure and be able to focus more on Michael Kors.

Prada: pros and cons of the purchase

The biggest pro of this acquisition is quite evident: Prada bought Versace at a bargain price compared to its potential. The two parties had reached an agreement for $1.6 billion, but following the widespread fear caused by Trump's tariffs, Versace's value dropped by another $200 million. As mentioned, Versace had been purchased for $2.1 billion in 2018.

In my opinion, Prada's decision to purchase this brand at this particular moment in time is not accidental, but part of a very specific strategy. The management has been very clever in exploiting the current market panic to obtain a considerable discount on the purchase price. Clearly, Versace will have to stop reporting operating losses to be considered a successful acquisition, but it has all the credentials to succeed.

In the high fashion industry, not many brands have a competitive advantage because it takes decades to cement consumer perception. Versace is a brand that was created in the late 70s, has a history of success behind it, and is still very popular despite everything. At $1.37 billion, I don't think Prada could have bought a better brand at the moment, and the potential return on investment is very high in my opinion.

With Versace, Prada is starting to become a true Italian giant of high fashion, as it also owns the Church's and Miu Miu brands. It can now play on equal terms with Kering (OTCPK:PPRUF) and give the LVMH group some tough competition.

Among other things, despite the negative period for the entire industry, its brands have all shown sales growth, which demonstrates how competent the management is. Miu Miu has almost achieved triple-digit growth and is a brand that is becoming increasingly premium.

Unlike Capri, which seems better suited to developing the potential of mid-range brands, Prada is more effective at exploiting high-end brands. For this reason, I am quite optimistic about this acquisition. Furthermore, the addition of Versace will improve revenue diversification and reduce the excessive concentration on the Prada brand.

In the initial stages Versace will drag down operating income, but this should only be a temporary problem. Once integrated into the company, the benefits should be considerable. Clearly, there is no guarantee that Versace will return to its former glory and this is the main risk.

Prada's management is very competent, but this is not a risk-free investment; a huge amount of work is needed to ensure that Versace starts being profitable again with double-digit margins. Consider that Prague and Versace are totally different from a stylistic point of view: the former aims for minimalism, the latter is eccentric and has a thousand nuances.

Conclusion

Overall, I believe this deal is beneficial for both companies:

  • Capri Holdings gets rid of a brand that it failed to make the most of, and with the proceeds it will reduce its debt and invest in its core brand.
  • Prada buys an internationally renowned brand at a discount and will integrate it into an already strong portfolio.

If I had to choose who benefited the most, I would say Prada. The timing of the acquisition was perfect, because the entire sector is in crisis and Trump's tariffs have fueled panic. Versace's value has dropped significantly, and for only $1.37 billion it has added a brand with great potential to its portfolio. A potential turnaround could make Prada an even bigger giant in the industry than it already is, and definitively detach Kering's competition.

The acquisition of Versace marks another step in the evolutionary journey of our Group, adding a new dimension, different and complementary. The Group’s infrastructure is strong, we have verticalized our brands’ organizations and reinforced our routines and processes. We feel ready to open this new chapter. Versace has huge potential. The journey will be long and will require disciplined execution and patience. The evolution of a brand always needs time and constant focus. I would like to thank Capri Holdings for having preserved and enhanced the heritage of this wonderful brand. Notwithstanding the sector uncertainties, we look at the future with confidence, focused on a long-term strategic vision.

Andrea Guerra, Group Chief Executive Officer.

There is a risk of failing, but the safety margin is high given the low price paid. The acquisition is expected to close in the second half of 2025, but regulatory approval is still pending. Should it be approved, Prada is ready to carve out an even bigger space for itself in high fashion and Capri Holdings is poised to make a strong commitment to Michael Kors.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Two High Yielding REITs Going From Bargains To Screaming Buys
data.description:
One of the benefits of the recent market selloff is the emergence of new opportunities to deploy capital at higher yields.

The logic is simple, the lower the share price, the higher yield provided that the underlying dividend remains unchanged. The last part of that sentence is critical as, theoretically, we should appreciate the market's rationality and that it prices the assets based on the future cash flows. So, having higher yielding opportunities now should mean that the risk level in the system has gone up (alternatively, the future cash flows streams have decreased due to, say, increased costs, lowered demand etc.).

In my view, all of this is fair and it is only logical that the tariffs have pushed down the valuations for almost all equities out there.

Yet, there are areas and specific securities that before the tariff risk materialized were trading at already relatively depressed valuations.

One such an asset class is equity REITs. Before tariff-induced roller coasters started, REITs, in general, were dealing with inflation and interest rate uncertainty that held the valuations back from a decent recovery. Plus, the market was so consumed with the growth-biased and "risk-on" assets that many undervalued REITs remained simply under the radar.

What is interesting is that even with growth and the S&P 500 (SPY) trading at very rich multiples, the real estate market (VNQ), which was far from being overvalued, has still suffered heavier losses.

One major reason for this is the surge in the 10-year yield volatility that makes it quite difficult for REITs to a) embark on accretive M&As (as the stabilized WACC is unknown) and b) model new debt service cash flows that would come from bond refinancings.

Plus, as the REIT universe is rather heterogeneous, there are specific sectors that have pushed the VNQ down way more than other.

Two of them include hotels and health care REITs. And in these sectors, by being extremely selective, we can scope some very enticing buying opportunities.

Here are two REITs (1 health care and 1 hotel/lodging), which, in my view, have gone from bargains to screaming buys.

#1 Global Medical - 12.0%

Global Medical (NYSE:GMRE) is a small-cap medical office building REIT that has been punished very hard by the market. Currently, it trades at a P/FFO of 8.1x, which is the lowest level it has had except for the COVID-19 period. The multiple has been already depressed going into the Trump's Presidency.

In the chart below, we can see how huge the difference between GMRE and VNQ there has been since the Fed started to hike the interest rates.

On a YTD basis, GMRE has also underperformed VNQ by ~300 basis points.

So, what are the main reasons for this?

First, GMRE as a small-cap REIT is per definition deemed riskier than large cap ones that can access debt financing at better and more flexible terms.

Second, most of the properties are spread across the secondary and tertiary markets, which, theoretically, increase the risk.

Third, many investors draw pareales to Medical Properties Trust (NYSE:MPW), which has been a complete disaster due to two major tenants going belly up.

Fourth, the underlying fundamentals are not best-in-class.

Fifth, and this is really summing the four pieces above together and contextualizing them with the prevailing market dynamics: i.e., assuming risk in the last thing that many investors want now.

While I agree that GMRE should deserve a below-average P/FFO multiple, P/FFO of 8.1x seems just wrong and overly aggressive.

Here is why.

Even though GMRE is a small-cap REIT it is well-diversified, owning 190 building that are distributed across 37 States. The lion's share of GMRE's medical office buildings are leased out on either triple-net or absolute lease basis that help the REIT avoid headwinds on the OpEx front.

Since the IPO, GMRE's portfolio has always remained strongly occupied with very limited provisions for non-performing tenants. As of now, the occupancy is at 96.4%. Speaking of the portfolio, we have to factor in the portfolio rent coverage, which is a clear indicative of a high-quality tenant base. Namely, the portfolio rent coverage is 4.5x and it has trended upwards over the past couple of quarters.

In the text above, I mentioned that GMRE has not the greatest fundamentals out there. It is because of two reasons:

  1. AFFO payout ratio of 95%
  2. Fixed rate 2.8% debt maturity in mid 2026.

So, the AFFO payout ratio looks quite high and given the 2026 debt maturity, if things do not improve, we could make a case for a dividend cut.

However, GMRE had a couple of similar debt refinancing events in 2023 and 2024 period, and the AFFO per share remained perfectly stable. The thing is that GMRE enjoys annual rent escalators of ~2%, which in combination with accretive asset recycling activity stimulate the AFFO growth nicely. For example, in Q4, 2024 GMRE sold ~$40 million of assets at a low 7% cap rate (in early 2024 it had sales at 5% cap rate). Then GMRE took these proceeds and combined with an additional debt to acquire a ~$69 million asset at a cap rate of 9%. This is how GMRE can create and/or preserve value, while issuing fresh equity is not an option at these valuations.

In a nutshell, GMRE is higher quality REIT than what we can, currently, imply from the market (i.e., the multiple). Sure, the dividend is not the safest out there, but at a P/FFO of 8.1x, 12% yield and the aforementioned fundamentals, GMRE is a bargain.

#2 Apple Hospitality - 8.9%

Apple Hospitality (NYSE:APLE) is a pure-play hotel/lodging REIT, which trades at a similar dirt cheap multiple as GMRE. Currently, the P/FFO stands at 7.3x and the dividend yield is 8.9%.

While APLE's valuations have never been high, what we have seen happening since mid February is a complete meltdown in the market cap. APLE has underperformed VNQ by ~19% on a YTD basis. It is down ~25% this year.

Here, the logic behind the plunge is simple.

The key driver for APLE's depressed share price is the assumption of an recessionary environment and the knock-on effects to the economy. The idea is that if we are in the recession, hotels will be one of the first items, where both corporates and individuals will try to turn light off in order to save some cash flows.

I do not think that we have something to argue against such a scenario (even though it is still unclear whether we will really experience a painful and/or prolonged recession).

It is completely normal to see cyclical stocks like hotel REITs get hammered under such uncertainty levels that we have now.

Having said that, just as I think that the market has gone too far by penalizing GMRE at this extent, in APLE's case the situation is, in my opinion, the same.

Here is why.

First, APLE runs a rooms-focused business model, which is, generally, more defensive than the competing strategies. Plus, it tends to be less CaPex and OpEx-intensive, which, in turn, offers a greater margin of safety to handle the pressures on the cash flows.

Second, an additional layer of defense comes from the fact that APLE's portfolio is quite fresh and exposed to favorable supply/demand dynamics. For instance, the average effective age of APLE buildings is ~5 years. The risk of new supply weakening the positioning of APLE's portfolio is the lowest it has been since 2019.

Third, APLE has, arguably, one of the best financials in the hotel REIT sector. The net debt to total capitalization is only 28%. Net debt to EBITDA is around 3.0x, which is much lower than what we can see for other (even blue-chip REITs) - i.e., usually, between 5.0x to 6.0x.

Finally, APLE carries a robust cash flow (MFFO) payout ratio of 61%. In my opinio, this should be more than enough to absorb any forthcoming declines that might arise from a weaker demand. In fact, I would not be surprised if APLE embarks on accretive share buybacks, using the undistributed MFFO and some prudent amount of leverage to acquire its stock at P/FFO of 7.2x.

The bottom line

The heightened volatility has created interesting opportunities across the board. The REIT universe is particularly attractive as it has both lagged behind the S&P 500 before the tariffs and continued to underperform after they got announced.

In this article, I have discussed two 9%+ yielding REITs, which are backed by fundamentals that are much stronger than what the valuations multiples indicate.

data.title: DigitalOcean: Don't Miss The Pivot
data.description:
The DigitalOcean (NYSE:DOCN) investment thesis hinges two critical factors. Firstly, a renewed leadership team that’s driving a product led growth strategy combined with a push for higher net-retention-rate through innovative solutions in the SMB market. The second factor is a meaningful margin acceleration, which combined with strong growth will likely lead to 27% IRR in 2027 or 60% upside from current trading levels.

Overview of the business

Established in 2012, Digital Ocean’s core mission is to simplify cloud computing and artificial intelligence for developers and tech companies. Its cornerstone offering called Droplet is a virtual machine that offers flexibility to build, test, secure, grow and scale applications on the cloud. Droplets are essentially Linux-based virtual machines that run on top of virtualized hardware. Each Droplet you create is a new server, either standalone or as part of a larger, cloud-based infrastructure.

The company focuses on simplicity and ease of use. Different configurations are optimized for different needs according to the scope in question - such as general purpose computing, CPU intensive tasks, memory optimization or storage optimization. Over time, the management team has strategically expanded its portfolio through some organic product innovation and M&A activity, such as Paperspace and Cloudways , to offer a comprehensive suite of cloud services spanning Infrastructure as a Service , Platform as a Service, Software as a Service, and machine learning & AI solutions.

Inherent to its product led growth strategy is its self service platform whereby customers , primarily developers in the SMB field, can manage cloud resources with easy of access and transparent through a predictable monthly consumption model. Unlike the major hyper-scalers such as Amazon’s AWS, Microsoft’s Azure, and Alphabet’s GCP, which provide a massive array and often overwhelming selection of products and features for enterprise level usage, DOCN focuses on serving core functionality, infrastructure and platform needed for SMBs. The so called “hyper-scaler lite” doesn't mean a lack of capability but a laser focus on simplicity and accessibility of usage. This simplicity offer superior total cost of ownership economics for customers.

DOCN’s geographical sales footprint is split into 38% US, 28% Europe, 24% Asia and 10% Rest of World. My assumptions in the proceeding valuation framework are underpinned by a series of arguments that I lay out below.

Growth

To understand the future, we need to have a decent understanding of what happened in the past. Coming out of the COVID digital boom, DigitalOcean enjoyed a big wave of organic growth from its frictionless product led growth strategy. The customer-centric development mindset fuelled solid growth of new customers and the installed base grew healthily to now reach 638K. Most customers land on the simplest tier and then it’s all about landing and expanding. However, after the headwinds in the tech world of 2022, organic growth decelerated from 20s% in the previous years to 7% and Net Dollar Retention Rates troughing at 96.3% in Q3 23. Besides the macro headwinds, management also acknowledged that they underinvested for a period of time in product innovation, particularly in features required by scaling or scaled customers which further contributed to the dip. The cherry on the cake was the lapsing comps of price increases that happened in 2022.

The next chapter of this growth began with the strategic pivot initiated by the new CEO Paddy Srinivasanstory appointed in Feb 2024. Since then, the focus is on "double-click and deliver on our core cloud computing platform" and to "jumpstart AI" (UBS conference). As part of that, the new CEO put heavier emphasis on product innovation, especially for scaler customers, spending over $25K annually, customers that were somewhat neglected in the past 2 years. This customer cohort collectively drives 36% of total revenue in Q4 24. High spend customers account for 88% of revenue and grew 6% YoY in Q424, while ARR grew 16% signalling good product uptake and organic growth.

Looking ahead in 2025, the company guides for full year revenue growth in the mid-teens with 13% being the midpoint. From the recent investor day, the it’s clear that internal momentum is strong despite potential macro headwinds. Net dollar retention ("NDR") is on the rise, hitting 99% in Q4 24 and even stronger at 100% for core cloud. The re-organization and initiatives are already paying off. The NDR target for 2025 is 100% and I foresee management beating that target as the scalers cohort of customers continues to grow fast. The early adoption of their GPU droplets and GenAI platform is dictating their differentiated, inference-focused strategy.

Much like many other product led growth companies, the plan over the medium term is to start landing bigger deals on day 1. As the company is developing enterprise-grade features such as advanced networking, it is making a compelling total cost of ownership argument as its at-least 30% cheaper than hyper-scalers according to their insights share in the investor April investor day. I expect organic growth to reaccelerate to 18-20% by 2027 driven by new customer acquisition and healthy expansion with the inflection in NDR outlined above. There are already strong signs in the large customer cohorts with NDR improving over 18%.

Over the next five to seven years, I expect organic growth in the mid teens coming from the vast potential hidden inside of the current and growing customer base. Digital ocean’s focus on reducing complexity, providing ease of use and reducing total cost of ownership fits the market’s needs. Admittedly, they dropped the ball on product innovation and execution over the past couple of years but structural and organisational changes have been made to address these. DigitalOcean's long-term strategy is underpinned by a key pillar, that the cloud market, despite its vast size, is daunting and leaving customers behind due to increasing complexity and cost. The company’s cost effective and easy to use solution avoids direct confrontations and price wars against hyper-scalers. Even though competitors like Vultr compete on price and some performance metrics, DigitalOcean’s user experience, reliability and established developer community act as strong defences.

Margin profile and forecast

Looking back in late 2021, the company had a solid customer base and even raised prices. That made for a robust gross margin of roughly 63%. Coming out of the macro headwinds in 2022, the company posted 57% gross margins in 2023. During that period, customers optimized their cloud spend and tried to do more with less. In 2024, the arrival of new leadership and their initiatives re-accelerated gross margins to 60%.

Looking ahead, I foresee gross margins heading back to mid-60s% with the new Atlanta data-centre creating cost efficiencies, and their higher margin platform as a service products, that are projected to be roughly 30% of core cloud revenue, growing significantly faster than basic infrastructure. On top of that, the AI platform has stronger margins that simple GPU infrastructure which will provide an additional uplift. Management is also allocating efforts to boost the efficiency of current infrastructure that will likely lead to roughly 200bps of gross margin expansion.

On the operating front, I foresee adj. EBITDA holding strong in the low/mid 40s % while the company is investing in going up-market to reaccelerate organic revenue growth which will eventually lead to operating leverage. As the capex outlays for AI infrastructure start to ease, I foresee the free cashflow margin going high teens or low 20s. To sum up the margin story, it’s one that evolved from a mis-prioritized management and imperfect efficiency to one of higher efficiency, targeted investments in high value pockets like PaaS and AI, and infrastructure optimization.

Model and risks

The assumptions for my modelling have been explained in the preceding body of text. In fact my expectations align with consensus figures for this year and 2026, but diverge slightly from consensus on the upside for both growth and profitability for the combined rationale outlined above. Regarding the selected exit multiple, I prefer to be conservative and model for 13x which is just a touch below the two-year average of 13.8x and well below the 17x reached at peak. With that, my model leads me to a 27% IRR and roughly 60% return from current levels.

The first and most important risk to discuss is competition. Although DigitalOcean focuses on SMBs, hyper-scalers are always a force to be reckoned and should not be neglected just because of the TCO argument. In the unlikely case that they choose to simplify down their offerings and cut their prices, they could effectively steal DOCN's right to win. Management is not trying to out-muscle the hyper-scalers but rather focus on their own value proposition while innovating on the edges to mitigate this risk.

Execution risk is always inherent with strategic pivots and management changes such as this one. A product-led growth strategy in combination with an up-market push can be tricky to navigate. However, I believe the new leadership team is sufficiently capable to execute especially considering their background in leading companies such as Microsoft, Amazon and Oracle. We are also at a point in the cycle where companies are seeking to do more with less while keeping TCO in check. This bodes well with DigitalOcean's offering.

Finally, sentiment shifts such as the recent one can introduce significant volatility in the stock but also in deal flow as executive's confidence flows with general sentiment in the market. Despite that, DigitalOcean has weathered the storm of 2022 and is structurally well positioned to capitalize on the rise of AI and the rising wave of SMBs in this new era.
